title: Reading and Use of English - Part 8
description: Read the texts and match each question with the correct text (A-D).

texts.A: Oxford psychologist Charles Spence... [56]When Spence started studying the sensory science behind flavour perception, it was a deeply unfashionable subject.[/56] [52]He says that from ancient times, there was a notion that the senses involved in eating and drinking were less sophisticated[/52] than those of hearing and vision. Now, no one questions the validity of the research field he calls 'gastrophysics'. Spence heads the Crossmodal Research Laboratory at the University of Oxford. [49]'Crossmodal', here, is the investigation of how all the senses interact.[/49] Although we rarely realise it, when it comes to flavour perception, we all have synaesthesia.
texts.B: Take a perfectly ripe strawberry... [48]But if you've ever experienced the blandness of eating a strawberry while holding your nose, you'll believe[/48] the oft-quoted statistic that flavour is 80% down to smell. ... [54]There's a growing acceptance that we can also detect less obvious tastes such as metallic, fat, carbonation, water and calcium, among others.[/54]
texts.C: Furthermore, aroma is bound up with memory and emotion. [51]'The nerves relating to smell go directly to the amygdalae,' says Avery Gilbert[/51], a world authority on smell. 'These are areas of the brain involved in emotional response — fight or flight, positive and negative emotion.' ... [55]we learn to love the bitterness of coffee through sheer force of will (wanting to be grown up).[/55] ... [51]the brain has paired the aroma with the experience.[/51]
texts.D: When it comes to dinnerware, the heavier it is, the more viscous, creamy and expensive the food served is perceived to be. ... [50]There's little evidence as to why this is the case, but ingrained associations are often suggested.[/50] ... [47]Big food brands use these associations to surreptitiously increase appeal.[/47] ... [53]It can demystify appetite and flavour for everyone, inform and inspire us to eat well, while offering a window into the bigger picture of how our senses and minds work.[/53]

questions.question: mention that people are tempted to purchase certain foods without realising why?
questions.explanation: Text D says brands use associations 'surreptitiously' (secretly) to increase appeal.

questions.question: give an instance of flavour being suppressed?
questions.explanation: B mentions 'blandness' when eating a strawberry while holding the nose.

questions.question: define what a term means in a specific context?
questions.explanation: A defines 'Crossmodal' as the investigation of how all senses interact.

questions.question: say some effects cannot yet be fully explained?
questions.explanation: D states there is 'little evidence' for why dinnerware weight affects us.

questions.question: give a physical explanation for a close connection?
questions.explanation: C explains smell nerves go directly to the amygdalae (physical connection).

questions.question: emphasise how long a prejudice has existed?
questions.explanation: A says the notion of taste being 'less sophisticated' exists 'from ancient times'.

questions.question: assert that there are multiple benefits to recent findings about taste?
questions.explanation: D lists benefits like demystifying appetite and inspiring healthy eating.

questions.question: say that the ability to perceive a wide range of tastes is increasingly being acknowledged?
questions.explanation: B mentions the 'growing acceptance' of detecting tastes like metallic or fat.

questions.question: claim people make an effort to acquire a liking for something?
questions.explanation: C mentions learning to love coffee through 'sheer force of will'.

questions.question: say few people used to be interested in examining the senses associated with taste?
questions.explanation: A says studying sensory science was 'deeply unfashionable' when Spence started.
